Malakoplakia is a classic urinary-tract inflammatory mimic characterized by histiocytic sheets and Michaelis-Gutmann bodies.
Malakoplakia belongs in the bladder mimic slide because it can produce a mass-forming lesion and is defined by histiocytic sheets with Michaelis-Gutmann bodies.
BCG therapy can produce granulomatous cystitis that looks dramatic at low power. Always correlate with treatment history before overcalling infection or new tumor.
On higher power, the lesion reads as granulomatous inflammation rather than urothelial atypia. This is a useful counterexample when discussing post-therapy specimens.
At low power, PUNLMP retains architectural order with slender papillae and lacks the crowded, disorganized fusion pattern of high-grade papillary carcinoma.
At high power, PUNLMP shows only mild nuclear enlargement and largely preserved polarity rather than the pleomorphism and mitotic activity expected in carcinoma.
Bladder CIS is a flat, high-grade lesion. The architecture is not papillary; the key is diffuse atypia with loss of maturation across the urothelial thickness.
The high-power CIS view is the slide to use against reactive atypia: nuclei are enlarged, pleomorphic, hyperchromatic, and disordered through the full thickness.
Inverted urothelial papilloma is a benign endophytic lesion. It earns a place in the bladder lecture because it helps frame the difference between orderly inverted growth and infiltrative nests.
